skill.name: ai-native-development
skill.description: Build AI-native applications with RAG pipelines, vector databases, function calling, agent workflows, streaming responses, and AI observability patterns.
skill.summary: Guides teams through practical AI application architecture, RAG, agents, tools, and production monitoring.
skill.author: AI Agent Hub

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, template strings, API documentation links, and normal SDK environment-variable configuration. No prompt injection attempt, malicious exfiltration, or hidden command execution intent was found. The main residual risk is unsafe copy-paste sample code, especially an eval-based calculator tool and broad autonomous-agent tool templates.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe eval-based calculator example
medium_findings.description: The agent workflow reference defines a calculator tool that returns eval(expression). This is dangerous if copied into an agent because model-controlled or user-controlled input could execute arbitrary JavaScript. The surrounding context is educational reference material, so this is not evidence of malicious intent. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for unsafe sample code. confidence: 0.94. confidence_reasoning: Direct eval() is present in a tool handler, and the semantic context shows the expression comes from tool input. Risk is reduced because it is documentation, not hidden runtime code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct eval() is present in a tool handler, and the semantic context shows the expression comes from tool input. Risk is reduced because it is documentation, not hidden runtime code.

medium_findings.title: Autonomous tool templates need authorization gates
medium_findings.description: The agent workflow template demonstrates web search, database query, and email tools that can be selected by an LLM-driven loop. The sample implementations are placeholders, but production use would need authorization, confirmation for side effects, allowlists, and argument validation. Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW for safe integration controls. confidence: 0.78. confidence_reasoning: The template explicitly exposes side-effect-capable tools to an autonomous agent loop, but the functions are demonstrative placeholders rather than active malicious actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The template explicitly exposes side-effect-capable tools to an autonomous agent loop, but the functions are demonstrative placeholders rather than active malicious actions.

medium_findings.title: RAG context is inserted into prompts without explicit untrusted-context guard
medium_findings.description: The RAG template and chatbot example place retrieved document text and user messages directly into model messages. The system prompt restricts answers to context, but it does not explicitly instruct the model to treat retrieved content as untrusted and ignore instructions inside documents. Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW for prompt-injection resilience. confidence: 0.70. confidence_reasoning: The pattern is common and legitimate, but the sampled code lacks a clear document-instruction isolation rule, which is a known risk for RAG systems.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is common and legitimate, but the sampled code lacks a clear document-instruction isolation rule, which is a known risk for RAG systems.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and TypeScript template literals. The reviewed locations are documentation examples, not Ruby backtick execution or shell command invocation. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. confidence: 0.93. confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows code fences and template strings, and no child_process, exec, spawn, or shell invocation evidence was found in the sampled files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows code fences and template strings, and no child_process, exec, spawn, or shell invocation evidence was found in the sampled files.

low_findings.title: Environment-variable access is standard SDK configuration
low_findings.description: The env_access and secret findings reference SDK initialization with API keys from process.env. I found no evidence that these values are logged, written to files, or sent to unauthorized endpoints. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for credential theft, with normal secret-handling caution. confidence: 0.88. confidence_reasoning: The cited lines pass environment variables to OpenAI, Pinecone, Anthropic, or observability SDK clients, which is expected configuration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines pass environment variables to OpenAI, Pinecone, Anthropic, or observability SDK clients, which is expected configuration behavior.

low_findings.title: Path traversal and weak-crypto scanner hits are contextual false positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal hits are relative documentation imports or cross-skill references, and the weak-cryptography hits align with ordinary AI terminology such as embeddings, models, similarity metrics, and checklist headings. No evidence found of file reads, crypto implementation, or traversal against user-supplied paths. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. confidence: 0.86. confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations show imports, reference links, and checklist text rather than filesystem access or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations show imports, reference links, and checklist text rather than filesystem access or cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation and local service examples
low_findings.description: The network findings include vendor documentation links, localhost vector database endpoints, and an example weather API call. These are not covert destinations or exfiltration endpoints, but production code should encode URL parameters and use configured endpoints. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious networking. confidence: 0.84. confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples tied to the skill topic, and no secret material is sent to them in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples tied to the skill topic, and no secret material is sent to them in the reviewed context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic JavaScript evaluation in tool handler
dangerous_patterns.description: The calculator example uses eval(expression), which is a dangerous pattern for LLM tool execution. Replace it with a safe math parser or a strict expression evaluator. confidence: 0.94. confidence_reasoning: Direct eval usage is present at the cited line inside an agent tool example.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct eval usage is present at the cited line inside an agent tool example.

dangerous_patterns.title: Model-selected tools can perform side effects
dangerous_patterns.description: The template shows an LLM-driven loop executing tool calls, including email and database-style tools. Side-effecting tools should require policy checks, confirmation, and least-privilege credentials. confidence: 0.78. confidence_reasoning: The agent loop and tool definitions are clear, but side effects are placeholder implementations in sample code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The agent loop and tool definitions are clear, but side effects are placeholder implementations in sample code.

content.value_statement: AI applications need reliable retrieval, tool use, cost controls, and monitoring to work in production. This skill provides patterns and templates for RAG, agents, vector databases, streaming, and observability.
actual_capabilities: Explains RAG architecture, document chunking, retrieval, citations, and answer validation patterns.
Provides TypeScript templates for RAG pipelines and agentic workflows.
Covers vector database setup patterns for Pinecone, Chroma, Weaviate, and Qdrant.
Describes OpenAI and Anthropic function calling loops, tool schemas, and streaming tool use.
Includes observability, token tracking, cost monitoring, and evaluation guidance.
Supplies an implementation checklist for AI application readiness reviews.
limitations: Some code is illustrative and needs hardening before production use.
The skill does not provision cloud services or vector databases automatically.
Model names and vendor APIs may need updates as providers change versions.
Security controls such as authorization and secret rotation remain the implementer responsibility.

use_cases.title: Launch a support knowledge assistant
use_cases.description: Design a RAG chatbot with citations, retrieval validation, streaming responses, and cost tracking.

use_cases.title: Add tool use to an AI workflow
use_cases.description: Structure function schemas, agent loops, tool execution, and error handling for controlled automation.

use_cases.title: Review AI production readiness
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to evaluate monitoring, prompt quality, retrieval quality, security, and operating costs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic RAG App
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a small knowledge assistant
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AI-native development skill to outline a simple RAG application for my documents. Include chunking, vector storage, retrieval, answer generation, and citations.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Vector Database
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare database options for an implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Pinecone, Chroma, Weaviate, and Qdrant for my use case. Consider scale, hosting, filtering, latency, operations, and cost.

prompt_templates.title: Design Safe Tool Calling
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a function calling workflow for this task. Include tool schemas, validation, authorization checks, error handling, and confirmation for side effects.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an AI System Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my AI application architecture for retrieval quality, prompt injection risk, observability, token cost, latency, model fallback, and deployment readiness.

output_examples.input: Plan a customer support RAG assistant for product documentation.
output: A retrieval architecture with document ingestion, chunking, embeddings, vector indexing, query retrieval, grounded generation, and citations.
A readiness checklist covering answer validation, monitoring, rate limits, fallback behavior, and cost alerts.

output_examples.input: Design tool calling for an order status assistant.
output: A tool set with strict schemas for lookup operations and clear separation from side-effecting actions.
A control plan with authentication, input validation, audit logs, retry handling, and user confirmation before changes.

output_examples.input: Reduce latency and cost in an existing AI chatbot.
output: A prioritized optimization plan using model routing, prompt caching, token limits, batch processing, retrieval tuning, and usage monitoring.
A measurement plan for latency, token cost, retrieval precision, answer accuracy, and user feedback.

best_practices: Treat retrieved documents and tool outputs as untrusted context in prompts.
Require validation, authorization, and logging before executing model-selected tools.
Track retrieval quality, answer quality, latency, and token cost from the first prototype.
anti_patterns: Copying example agent tools into production without permission checks.
Using eval or unrestricted interpreters for calculator or automation tools.
Sending secrets, private records, or raw debug prompts into model context.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps build AI applications that use retrieval, embeddings, tools, agents, streaming responses, and observability.

faq.question: Does it include runnable production code?
faq.answer: It includes templates and examples, but they need project-specific hardening, testing, and deployment work.

faq.question: Which AI providers does it discuss?
faq.answer: It covers OpenAI and Anthropic patterns, with general concepts that also apply to other LLM providers.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review tool permissions, prompt injection defenses, secret handling, observability, rate limits, cost controls, and model fallback behavior.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: Most static findings are false positives, but some examples show unsafe patterns that require caution before copying into production.
